lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Not everything is a package deal. Sometimes truth is a choice — **this** or **that**.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Disjunction. $P \\lor Q$. It is enough that one of them is true — you need not say which.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Introduction is easy: if you have $P$, then `(or-intro-l N F)` gives you $P \\lor F$ for any $F$. Likewise `(or-intro-r N F)` from the right.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Elimination is the interesting one. You have $P \\lor Q$. You do not know which. So you must handle **both** possibilities and show that either way, the same conclusion follows.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: `(or-elim N M K)` — [proof-by-cases|proof by cases]. Step $N$ is the disjunction. Step $M$ proves the conclusion assuming $P$. Step $K$ proves the same conclusion assuming $Q$.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A fork in the road that rejoins at the same destination.
